# Sandstream

> Peter Sandström — hands-on tech lead and consultant (AI, product, digital transformation), Stockholm. Sandstream is the umbrella for his tools and writing. The flagship open-source project is **kit**.

## kit — developer environment manager

kit is a deterministic, local-first developer-environment manager for AI agents and humans. One command takes a repo from `git clone` to a working dev environment: it installs and pins tools (via mise), runs guided logins, materializes secrets from a vault, wires agent config, and runs a security gate. Runs on macOS, Linux, and natively on Windows. Current release: 3.2.0 — kit 3 takes it from a provable local floor to an org-governed control plane (machine identity + signable policy-as-code + governed cross-device memory), all additive over 2.x. 3.1–3.2 add GitHub Copilot rules-file support, public-key memory sync (an ephemeral session pushes with no secret), `kit memory learn`, atomic PAL claims for parallel agents, and a Sentrux plugin that gates architectural decay.

kit is **not another scanner** — it orchestrates and normalizes the scanners you already use (Semgrep, Snyk, Trivy, Grype, osv-scanner, plus opt-in GuardDog malware detection and its own supply-chain checks) into one deterministic gate with cited findings. Cloud-only tools that can't run air-gapped surface as an honest skip, never a false green. It does not rebuild SAST.

Key properties:
- Deterministic and local-first — no LLM in the core; zero telemetry.
- Provable green — a crashed or missing scanner fails the gate instead of exiting 0; a signed `kit check --attest` receipt and provable air-gap (`kit airgap verify`) record which scanners actually ran, verifiable offline.
- Stable contracts — the CLI, `.kit.toml` config schema, and adapter SDK are versioned, tiered, and drift-tested. The promise held across the 3.0 major: additive over 2.x, no stable command removed or downgraded (enforced by a public-surface invariant).
- Identity + signable policy-as-code (3.0) — a local Ed25519 `kit identity`, a signable, org-distributable `.kit-policy.toml` any kit verifies offline before enforcing (anchored to a committed `.kit-policy.signers` trust file), and `kit panic` to rotate + emit a signed revocation. Identity + policy is the contract.
- Tamper-evident, attributable audit (3.0) — the HMAC anchor binds attribution (kid/sig) into the seal (anchor v3), so stripping or forging the signer of a sealed entry is caught; `kit audit anchor --external` folds a third-party receipt (RFC3161 TSA / notary) into the seal, `--require-external` can demand it. No network client shipped.
- Per-CLI context lock — verifies each tool (gcloud, vercel, github, git, npm) points at the declared account + project, and never infers the pairing from whatever happens to be logged in. A right account with the wrong project is a mismatch, not a pass.
- Cross-harness memory — indexes transcripts from Claude Code, Codex, Gemini, Continue, Cursor, Amazon Q, Cline, and OpenCode (8 agents) into one local SQLite store, so agent state is portable across tools. Governed cross-device memory: `kit memory push`/`pull` sync your private store over your own encrypted transport (private git remote or a command transport like S3/rclone/scp/USB; gzip inside AES-256-GCM; opt-in; no cloud ledger); public-key mode (X25519) lets an ephemeral session push with no secret; PAL items are device-coupled (with atomic `pal claim` for parallel agents); and `kit memory learn` surfaces instructions you keep re-typing.
- Install-gate — a fail-closed pre-tool gate blocks an un-triaged package install across 7 agents (Claude Code, Codex, Amazon Q, Gemini CLI, Cursor, OpenCode, Cline). `kit agent-config` also writes the "use kit" rules block into 5 rules files (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md / .cursorrules / .clinerules / .github/copilot-instructions.md — the last added for GitHub Copilot in 3.1; Antigravity is covered via AGENTS.md).
- Local Ed25519 identity — `kit identity` gives each machine/agent an attributable signing key; a verifier needs only the public key.
- Multi-vault secrets — 12 secret backends resolve. 8 are writable vaults (1Password, Infisical, HashiCorp Vault, AWS/GCP/Azure secret managers, Doppler, dotenvx) that also migrate plaintext → vault, propagate to deploy targets, and rotate; the other four (Bitwarden, EAS, config, env) resolve read-only.
- MIT-licensed, Node 22+, published to npm as `sandstream-kit` with SLSA provenance, and as a signed (cosign), multi-arch Docker image `sandstream/kit` with a CycloneDX SBOM.

## About

- https://www.sandstre.am — Peter Sandström, tech lead and consultant. Ideas every day, code every day.
